answer:
\boxed{B}

The Spanish likely insisted on control of territory west of the line because they feared Portugal would claim their newly discovered lands (e.g., the Americas) as its own, undermining their own colonial ambitions. The treaty was a diplomatic compromise to resolve competing claims after Columbus’s voyages, ensuring Spain retained exclusive rights to the "New World" west of the demarcation. Portugal, in turn, secured rights to Africa and Asia east of the line.
